old_claim.note: Scientific Illustration Printing is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: Anatomical theater design is the same-year built-form context for public anatomy theaters, replacing printed scientific illustration as the direct venue concept.

source_shape.source_locator: The University of Padua heritage page dates the fixed anatomical theatre to 1594/1595 and describes it as a permanent structure created to teach anatomy through dissection.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes the built anatomical theater at Padua as a permanent dissection-teaching structure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That architecture is the same-year built-form context for the public venue; printed scientific illustration is related teaching media but not the direct venue concept.

ontology_before: Public Anatomy Theaters depended on Scientific Illustration Printing, conflating printed anatomical media with the public dissection venue.
ontology_after: Public Anatomy Theaters depend on Anatomical Theater Design as same-year built-form context and no longer directly depend on scientific illustration printing.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: public_anatomy_theaters now depends on anatomical_theater_design and no longer directly depends on scientific_illustration_printing.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to printed anatomy teaching aids rather than public dissection venues.
A stronger source showed printed scientific illustration was structurally required for the first public anatomy theaters.
Anatomical Theater Design were removed or merged into Public Anatomy Theaters, requiring a different direct built-environment prerequisite.
short_reason: Anatomy theaters need a dissection-theater venue; printed illustrations are related but not the direct built-form prerequisite.
